sentences.text: My name is Mila Bachner.
It's M-I-L-A B-A-C-H-N-E-R.
Well, I was born Mila Zagorski.
It's Z-A-G-O-R-S-K-I.
No.
Mirele.
M-I-R-E-L-E.
Mirele.
Chrzanow.
C-H-R-Z-N-O-W.
Poland.
P-O-L-A-N-D.
There was a population of about 22,000.
I would say half-- 11,000 Jews and 11,000 non-Jews.
Yes.
I had two brothers and two sisters.
I was the-- I was in the middle.
I had a younger sister and a younger brother.
And I had an older brother-- two older brothers.
And then-- and my sister-- Yeah.
It's-- it was Malka.
She was the oldest.
She was about 16.
And my brother Chaim who was about maybe 14.
And my brother [?
Murray ?] who was 14 and a half, my sister Mesia, who was 10 years old, myself-- I was, at the time, 12 and a half, and my brother Nahman who was about eight.
At the time of the war, yes.
Lieb Zagorski.
L-I-E-B Z-A-G-O-R-S-K-I and [?
Channa ?] Lemberger, L-E-M-B-E-R-G-E-R.
You know, I was a young child.
And I was more interested in playing games with my friends.
But I do know that there were five brothers.
One brother, Uncle David, lived in-- in Berlin with his wife.
And the three remaining brothers in Poland and my dad, had like a-- I know that there were always a lot of horses.
So I take it that they were dealing with horses.
Like business.
Yes.
I can describe my house.
It was a beautiful little apartment.
We were eight people in a two-and-a-half room apartment.
But the love and the closeness that I encountered in my young life, I don't think can ever be replicated.
We didn't have much luxuries, but what I had at home, no luxuries can take place.
We had love, respect, and the whole family.
I went to camp as a child.
I used to go to day camp and away for the-- for the summer.
I had my-- my-- Mommy would sew our clothes.
She would bake delicious breads and cakes.
We conducted magnificent Shabbat-- [?
Shabbosem ?] at home-- full of Yiddish guide, full of love for God and for people, and for mankind.
I came from public school and I went to Bais Yaakov, and I spend another two, three hours there.
I-- we had friends.
We had family.
We had many relatives at home and abroad.
My bubbe, my zayde, my aunts, my uncles, my cousins-- we used to go to their homes, especially on Shabbat, where my Uncle Joseph would test us in what we learned in Bais Yaakov over the week.
And he would test us in Chumash and reading the Torah.
It was a beautiful life.
It was poor in luxuries but rich in love.
Very close.
Yes, very much so.
Religion played an integral part in our life.
I think that my parents paid the rabbis to teach us-- to teach us Torah where we-- when we needed the money to buy bread.
It's as simple as that.
It was like mandatory.
And-- and I think in every Jewish home.
But to be poor, you would think that you buy the food first.
But not so.
They-- they-- the knowledge and the education was very important very-- and-- and Yiddish [? guide. ?] And-- and this is the way it was.
I loved every minute of it.
I still read Yiddish, and I write Yiddish and I speak Yiddish.
And-- and I love it.
I wouldn't have it any other way.
So do my children.
Each one of them speaks Yiddish.
They speak Hebrew.
They speak Yiddish.
And I gave them what my parents gave me.
And my time in America when I-- was not so easy.
My husband was very green.
You know.
And he made a-- he hardly made a living.
But the children came and Yeshiva was my priority.
So I worked.
I worked for various jobs I did, like bazaars, lunch [? mother ?] and schlepping things.
For the Hillel Academy, if I cannot pay full tuition, I made it up with work also.
And I love it.
I loved it and I love it.
Back in Poland, was Yiddish spoken in the home?
Yes.
Polish in school.
Yes.
There were incidents sporadically.
Like I had many non-Jewish friends, of course.
You know, you go to a school, to a public school, in you go with non-Jewish people also.
But I played beautifully with them.
We never had anything.
But I did hear anti-semitic remarks like, [INAUDIBLE], for instance.
He was my-- a neighbor of ours.
They owned a house.
And he-- I remember him once saying to me, [SPEAKING POLISH], which means, to Palestine with you Jews.
And I stood there, a child maybe eight years old-- maybe not even eight-- and I would say to him, give me Palestine and you'll see how quickly I'll go.
As a child, I was also very Zionistically inclined.
I leaned toward Israel, Palestine.
I think that all of them.
It [? hits ?] us all, you know?
This is something that's in the-- in the Torah, since the beginning of history-- that the Lord-- that God promised us Palestine.
And one day, he said, you are going to have it.
And we do.

And I remember saying this to him, as young as I was, I remember the songs that we sang in Polish about Israel-- in Polish about Palestine, about [INAUDIBLE] about-- I knew about the Balfour Declaration.
I knew all these things.
We-- we learned.
And I use to read.
Books were available.
Polish.
I would read about-- on [INAUDIBLE].
I would read on-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- the [INAUDIBLE] in is the-- You know.
Also a beautiful book.
I got this from my teacher, my Hebrew teacher, as a token of appreciation for my beautiful Hebrew that I spoke.
And I spoke fluent Hebrew.

I did and then, then during the Nazi years, the beatings, and the-- and the-- the troubles that we suffered-- the pain and the suffering-- it zapped us of everything, including the-- We were afraid to use anything, you know, Jewish, although they knew that we were Jewish.
I know I'm Jewish.
But we were told-- it's-- it's amazing how I remember these-- these pictures in silhouette on the walls.
Where you see a skull-- like a skeleton-- and then you would hear, shh [GERMAN], be quiet, because the [INAUDIBLE] is listening-- your enemy is listening.
And they meant us.
You know.
They meant us.
They meant the Jews-- that we should not talk, because the enemy is listening in.
This was during the-- when the Nazis came in.
And so we were afraid to talk.
And there were times we were afraid to laugh and to cry.
And, you know, it was-- it was-- it was bad.
I was too young.
But I went to Bais Yaakov.
I was busy with school.
I was only about eight, nine years old. 10-- even 10, I was still a young child.
And who wanted to know about-- you wanted to play.
I-- I was-- I was a child.
I did.
Yes.
We had many friends in our-- I didn't go far from home.
I never dared to venture far from-- far from home.
I had my friends right on our-- on our street.
What do young girls do?
We used to take walks on Shabbos after shul after we ate.
You know.
Mom and Dad went to lie down a little bit, and the children went out of the house.
You know.
And it was a small apartment.
And Mommy and Daddy needed to make a little love too, after all.
You realize this after, you know?
I know now what they did.
You know.
Then I didn't know.
And they were two beautiful people-- gorgeous people.
And we went down to our zayde, you know, and he would give us, you know, [INAUDIBLE] you called it-- like gifts for Sabbath-- for the Sabbath, which means cake, cookies, whatever.
And we took walks to-- to the-- to the lake, you know, the children.
We didn't swim on Shabbos.
We wouldn't dare put on a bathing suit.
And we played with our friends.
And then we came home.
And I went to the store, and Mommy said to me, Mirele, go to-- [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
And I will never forget that.
And I went to him and I told him what Mommy said.
Never talk money, just tell him to give it to you.
And then Mommy paid the day or two later.
Oh yeah, Mommy sent me down to the grocery store to Mr.
Winselberg ?] was his name.
He was a religious Jew with a beard.
And I should get half a herring.
This was for [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] is.
Before-- before-- before we light the-- we-- before there's the-- the [?
Habdallah ?] we have [INAUDIBLE] which means, [INAUDIBLE] before the Sabbath ends.
And the piece of herring was very appropriate almost every Shabbos until-- until the horrible thing happened.
This was like a-- it was like a must in a Jewish home.
I should get a herring, a piece a [INAUDIBLE], piece of cake, sour milk.
And it was just beautiful.
And then the love, you know-- the sisters, the brothers.
Very good.
Very well.
Very well.
We-- we-- I don't ever remember fighting with my siblings.
I don't, because my sister was old.
Malka was the oldest.
She was like the little [INAUDIBLE], like the little house model, sort of, in the house.
She would clean.
She would do-- so we listened to her, because she kept this apartment spotless.
And we had wooden floors, you know, and no carpets.
So you had to lay down and scrub the floors on-- on your knees.
And it was beautiful, you know, so-- Then she brought her friends in, which were beautiful people-- she-- the cream of the crop.
She was a magnificent looking girl, my sister.
She was beautiful.
We had gorgeous six children.
I helped with housecleaning, yes.
I-- I remember my sister going to school there too.
My sister graduated [INAUDIBLE], which is equivalent to high school here.
She was lucky she-- she-- she graduated.
She was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like, when you graduate high school, the [INAUDIBLE] is like high school-- it's equivalent to college here in-- in education.
The education was very intense.
You can imagine that I am in the United States of America.
I came in 1947.
That makes a 48 years.
It will be 49 years in March coming, God willing.
And I speak still Polish fluently.
I write it and I read it.
And I don't-- I don't converse with anybody.
Sometimes I come into companies-- you know, a Polish girl who would do-- if I go to have the manicure and she's Polish.
And she admires how I speak-- how I speak Polish.
She can't get over what a rich vocabulary I have.
And I must say, that the education was very intense-- very intense.
I speak-- I speak Yiddish and I wrote Yiddish.
Whatever we did, we did well.
I didn't like math.
But this doesn't stop me from graduating beautifully, because I was excellent in everything else.
I would consider myself a good student.
I loved religion.
I loved-- I loved all kinds of religion.
I loved-- I was very-- I loved India-- the land of India.
And I must tell you, when I saw a story on [INAUDIBLE] Saturday.
I forgot his name, but he showed Bombay.
And he showed how poor these people live.
I cried.
And I will tell you why.
Coming from a poor home, I relate to these things.
I don't cast this aside.
And I say to myself, why-- and again I say to myself, why must people have so many children?
They have no doctors.
They have no food.
There's illness all around.
They get the epidemics.
They should try first to make something of themselves, you know, before they have all these children.
Then you watch these little things die.
And this is such a painful thing.
Those are such gorgeous little god-given children-- products of our love.
And look what is happening.
So I remember reading this book, [INAUDIBLE]-- [INAUDIBLE] was the name of the son-- of the mother and [INAUDIBLE] was the name of the sun.
I will make this very brief.
And this was a poem that we had to learn for the next day.
I did not have my lunch.
On my lunch hour, I learned this poem.
This is when I started reading it.
I could not get over this poem.
And I had to know it.
I know it to this very day.
And when did you study this poem?
In fourth grade.
Yes.
Because it described the Indian earth-- the earth of India-- the poverty, the hunger.
And this mother is holding this little boy in her hands-- that little [INAUDIBLE] boy.
And she sings a song to him.
And she tells him and this song how tremendously [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the poverty is there-- how-- how astronomic the poverty is-- no doctors.
And this child grows and grows and grows, until he becomes of age.
And when he says to her, Mama I cannot watch the poverty in our land.
I must go to-- to-- to the east.
And I want to become a doctor.
I'm going to study very hard.
So when I come back, I should be able to help my people.
And this sort of got me so, you know?
I was in the fourth grade, and I learned that poem and I know it to this very day.
And I watch today-- I watched this India-- and I thought to myself, this business [INAUDIBLE].
You know?
And so--
The poem-- it's-- I would have to translate it.
It's a long poem.
I don't want to take that much time with this.
It's just-- what I'm trying to bring out to you is that I was-- I always felt for the-- for the poor people, because I related to them.
Do you know?
You know, we had six children.
My dad made-- we-- he made a living, nothing to talk about.
No luxuries.
We thanked god for piece of bread and butter, potato, little borscht, a hamburger Mommy made.
For Shabbos, we had a chicken or a goose Mommy bought sometimes.
And we had that white tablecloth.
I, you know, I have visions.
I-- I-- pictures flash before my eyes, very much so.
And [INAUDIBLE], you know, the holidays-- the Pesach, the [INAUDIBLE], how we sat at the table-- my daddy in the white kittel.
And we conducted the Seder.
It was unbelievable.
And the four questions-- and the Haggadah.
And the--
I cried when I read the Haggadah then.
I'm crying when I read it now.
Because now we have-- we have an addition to the Haggadah-- our exodus, our flight to life.
But I think this was the worst-- the worst war there ever was.
I don't think any war can compare to that, because this was deliberate, it-- you know, willful, planned, slaughter of a nation, just because they did not like the Jewish people.
Why didn't they like us?
We brought a lot of culture into Germany-- doctors, professors, musicians, you name it.
Why?
Do you remember the first signs of the war?
I do.
It was in 19-- 1939.
It could have been the month of June.
I remember I came home.
It was-- it was like the last summer that I spent with me family together, you know.
I remember I came home.
My last day from school, and I told Mommy that what-- you know, how the girls are not talking to me-- the non-Jewish children.
I-- I felt like a pariah, like I was-- like pariahs, the Jewish children felt.
Because all of a sudden, you know, we-- after all, we did play together with them.
We went to school together.
We sat together on one bench.
We had benches in school, rather than chairs, you know, seats.
And we used to do our homework.
I was very helpful to this [INAUDIBLE].
We got along great.
I had a friend Sabina on my block.
We were like-- we were good friends.
I didn't know the difference.
People were people.
People are nice.
They're beautiful people.
Like I said, if people are not nice.
I don't bother with them.
But I was not a person that would hurt or insult.
You know, I would go home and say to Mommy, Mama, what happened to these-- to these girls?
Why don't they talk to me?
So Mommy said to me, Mila, don't worry about it.
Just be nice.
Everything is going to be all right.
I said OK.
You know.
And I ran out and I went about my business playing.
But it happened again and again, and until school ended already, the summer school, they didn't look at us.
And I came on crying to Mommy.
And I said Momma, there's something wrong.
I said, why don't these kids even look at me?
These are my friends.
We are school-- school-- you know, schoolmates.
Mommy said to me, Mirele, don't ask any questions.
And I said, Mama what is happening?
Then I look at Mommy and I see that she is-- you know, her face was like grayish.
My dad was-- you could see the depression in their faces.
And you know, all of a sudden, you hear that we cannot go to school anymore.
School had stopped for us until the-- we didn't finish anymore, the ending of the school.
We were-- even before the Nazis came in-- they already expelled us from school, the Jewish children.
So I never finished my fifth grade.
So you were in the fifth grade?
I was in the fifth grade.
Well, it-- in June-- I think June or beginning-- I don't know how it worked in-- in-- or beginning of July.
I think that school ended early July or late June, something.
And I-- and I remember, I said to Mommy-- I said, Mama, what is happening?
Why are you so upset?
Why-- why don't you look good?
And we didn't have any more-- we didn't have food in the house.
We could not go into a store anymore and buy food, because the-- the Gentiles would not sell it to us.
And the Jewish people could not get any more food into the stores.
They could anymore go buy.
So whatever they had, they probably saved for themselves.
And it left us, except if Mommy had flour, she would bake a cake or bread.
But it was very scarce.
And we were very religious, very strict.
My mommy wore a sheitel, you know.
So I remember my little brother, Nahman, you know, in the picture there-- he came in one day to the house.
And he says, Mommy, [?
Yanic ?] [INAUDIBLE], this was our neighbors in the backyard-- is eating bread with the [INAUDIBLE], the pig fat, that white stuff.
You know.
And he offered me a piece.
Mommy, I'm hungry.
What do you think I should do?
To my brother-- and Mommy looks at this child.
And she-- and I see that her eyes well up with tears.
And she says to him, [?
Nahman, ?] [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
Don't come and ask me any questions.
Do what you want.
She could not see the children be hungry.
And we have no food anymore.
So-- but he didn't go get it.
My brother was, at this point, he was eight or going to be eight.
He was just a little baby.
We didn't have anything to eat.
And then slowly but surely, once we knew-- we knew that school was no longer for us, they did not look at us, the non-Jewish people.
And I knew already, you know, I-- I just started sensing that something is wrong.
We started digging ditches in the backyard, by us-- in the garden.
We did, as children.
We were given shovels, for our lives, because everybody was glued to the-- we did not own a radio.
But there were people who could afford it so they owned the radios.
And we could hear the news coming over the radio that the German army is invading Poland.
And my mom, you know, and my dad lived through a war, you know, in the 1917 war.
And they knew what war can do to people.
So Mommy always told us, children, whatever will happen, you try to make it to a kitchen.
If you should be taken away from Dad and I, remember go work in a kitchen.
You'll have plenty of food.
Look how my mother were-- how my mommy's mind worked-- already worked then.
If they should take us from her, we should go and-- and work in a kitchen.
Like you could get it in the-- you could get into the kitchen as you wanted, you know?
